questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Which of the following, if true, most forcefully undermines the reasoning of the passage above?

choices.a: The cost of renting a video can essentially undermine substantially less than the price of a movie theatre admission.
choices.b: Most film production companies earn most of the income from the sale of video and movies.
choices.c: Fears of some that videos would completely supplant theatre movies have yet to come to pass.
choices.d: Since the start of the video boom, money spent on film entertainment other than videos and movies has increased.
choices.e: Entertainment other than movies and videos is released without being invested when released in video form.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: The use of petroleum products in the manufacture of plastics, closely regulated and limited by law. Our country's need for petroleum for the production is more vital than our need for plastics, and our dependence on foreign sources of petroleum could leave us more and more susceptible to the whims of foreign oil producers. Consequently, to rein in our access to these foreign countries by reducing our use of petroleum products in making plastics, would be major step toward national energy independence and our country's security. Which of the following, if true, would most greatly weaken the reasoning above?

choices.a: Only a small fraction of petroleum products consumed in this country is used in making plastics.
choices.b: Domestic sources of plastics manufacture can somewhat reduce the amount of petroleum needed.
choices.c: The development of atomic energy as an alternative to petroleum based energy has been slowed by legitimate concerns over safety.
choices.d: In times of war, combatant nations would be seriously tempted to forcibly fix the territories of petroleum-producing nations.
choices.e: Some plastic products, such as aircraft and motor vehicles, play an important role in our nation's defence.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Some scientists believe that, in certain species of birds, actual particles of metal within the brain react to the Earth's magnetic field in the same way that the needle in a compass. It is this mechanism that is thought to guide the birds' amazing ability to navigate accurately over great distances of thousands of miles by day and night during migration. To test this theory, researchers surgically removed the metal particles from the heads of some birds and then released them, alongwith a number of untreated birds at the usual time and place of their annual winter migration. Which of the following results would most seriously weaken this theory being tested?

choices.a: The experimentally treated birds and failed to migrate successfully.
choices.b: The surgically treated birds were able to follow their usual flight patterns successfully by day, but not by night.
choices.c: The surgically treated birds were able to migrate about as accurately as the untreated birds.
choices.d: The surgically treated birds were able to migrate successfully only when travelling in a group of untreated birds.
choices.e: In addition to being impaired in their migration ability, the surgically treated birds suffered a variety of other disabling effects.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: For our nation to compete successfully in the high-technology enterprises of the future, workers with skills in maths and science will be badly needed. But it is doubtful that they will be available, since there is a shortage of high school maths and science teachers has shown no signs of abating. This shortage in the industry can help alleviate this problem by funding scholarship grants and aid to college students who graduate in maths and science with the hope of pursuing teaching careers. Which of the following, if true, would most probably prevent the proposed plan from achieving its intended effect?

choices.a: After graduation from college, most maths and science graduates opt for jobs in industry rather than in teaching.
choices.b: Many high schools have been forced to lower their standards in hiring maths and science teachers.
choices.c: More scholarship money is already available for students of maths and science than is available for those in any other field.
choices.d: Population statistics show that the number of high school students is expected to decline over the next ten years.
choices.e: Many experts say that business of the future will require relatively few highly trained workers, along with many semi-skilled and purely technical employees.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Secondary school graduates in Japan score significantly higher on tests of science and mathematics than do students at the same level in the United States. Some educational reformers in the United States attribute this difference to the more rigid and vigorous Japanese secondary school programme, which emphasises required courses, long hours of study and homework, and memorisation to a far greater degree than do American schools. Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the conclusion drawn by the educational reformers cited?

choices.a: The Japanese elementary school programme is far less rigid and structured than the elementary school programmes in most United States schools.
